properties.name: El Capitan Bridge
properties.blurb: The bridge over the Merced just east of El Capitan. Short flat walk with the wall straight overhead.

properties.name: Church Bowl & Indian Creek Trail
properties.blurb: Quiet picnic meadow under the Royal Arches with the Indian Canyon trail climbing out the back.

properties.name: El Capitan Meadow
properties.blurb: Pull off Northside Drive at the meadow and look up.

properties.name: The Ahwahnee
properties.blurb: You don't have to be a guest. The Great Lounge and the back lawn are open to anyone.

properties.name: Sentinel Bridge South Side
properties.blurb: Half Dome catches the last light from the south end of the bridge.

properties.name: Muir's Glacial Erratic
properties.blurb: The boulder John Muir used as evidence the valley was carved by ice. Sits in the Merced. Look for the large feldspar chunks.

properties.name: Housekeeping Camp Swimming
properties.blurb: Sandy beach on the Merced behind Housekeeping Camp. Shallow, slow, and shaded by afternoon.

properties.name: Cascade Picnic Area
properties.blurb: Tables along Cascade Creek just inside the Arch Rock entrance. Easy stop on the way in or out.

properties.name: Trailhead Parking at Curry Village
properties.blurb: The day-use lot at Curry Village. Closest paid parking for the Mist Trail and the Happy Isles trailhead.

properties.name: Yosemite Lodge
properties.blurb: One of the best home bases for moving around the Valley. The lot fills up last because cars have to do the full loop before they end up parking here.

properties.name: The Fen
properties.blurb: One of the most peaceful areas of Yosemite Valley. Good spot for birding.

properties.name: Degnan's Deli
properties.blurb: Build-your-own deli sandwiches in Yosemite Village. Decent and filling for $10 to $12. The everyday lunch option when you don't want to drive out of the Valley.

properties.name: Curry Village Pizza Deck
properties.blurb: Outdoor pizza deck at Curry Village. Once decent, now genuinely good. The Half Dome is the standout.

properties.name: McGurk Meadow
properties.blurb: A 3.7-mile round trip off Glacier Point Road. One of the best wildflower hikes in the park; the old sheepherder's cabin partway in belongs to John McGurk. Wildflowers peak June through August.

properties.name: Lyell Canyon
properties.blurb: An 8-mile trail through a broad subalpine canyon with only 200 feet of gain. Starts across from the Dog Lake parking area. The further you go, the fewer people you see. Tioga Road season only.

properties.name: May Lake
properties.blurb: The best moderate hike in the high country. 1.2 miles each way and 500 feet of gain to an alpine lake under Mount Hoffmann, with views in every direction from the shoreline. The grade is steady but not steep, which makes it the right pick for pushing kids past where a flat walk would have ended. Swimming on warm afternoons, granite slabs on the east shore. Tioga Road season only.

properties.name: Gaylor Lake
properties.blurb: Steep but short. The trail leaves the Tioga Pass entrance station at 9,945 ft and climbs a little over a mile to a ridge at 10,500 ft, then drops into the Gaylor Lakes basin: granite shores, the Cathedral Range across the south, and the ruins of an 1880s silver mine on the slope above. The first half hour earns the rest. Best in late summer, July and August, once the snow is off the upper switchbacks.

properties.name: Chilnualna Falls Trailhead
properties.blurb: Wawona's serious hike. Eight miles round trip and 2,400 feet up to a chain of cascades that most Valley visitors never hear about. The lower falls are a quarter mile in if you only want a taste. Small lot at the end of Chilnualna Falls Road.

properties.name: Wawona Meadow Loop
properties.blurb: A flat 3.5-mile loop around the meadow on an old fire road, starting across from the Wawona Hotel. Wildflowers in late spring, almost nobody on a weekday. One of the few Yosemite trails where a stroller works.

properties.name: Pioneer Yosemite History Center
properties.blurb: Relocated historic cabins, a covered bridge over the South Fork, and the wagons that hauled visitors in before the roads were paved. Twenty minutes, free, and a good leg stretch between the south entrance and the Valley.

properties.name: Swinging Bridge Picnic Area (Wawona)
properties.blurb: Not the Valley's Swinging Bridge. This one crosses the South Fork east of the Wawona settlement, with granite slabs and swimming holes when the water drops in mid-summer. Locals' spot. Pack out everything.

properties.name: O'Shaughnessy Dam
properties.blurb: The dam that drowned the other Yosemite Valley. Walk across the top, through the tunnel blasted into the cliff, and look up-canyon at Kolana Rock and the falls. The whole reservoir story is written in the plaques.

properties.name: Wapama Falls Trail
properties.blurb: Five miles round trip from the dam along the reservoir's north shore to the footbridges under Wapama Falls. In big snowmelt years the spray soaks the bridges and the park closes them; in May it is the best waterfall walk nobody is on.

properties.name: Lookout Point
properties.blurb: A short climb from the Hetch Hetchy entrance road to a granite knob with the reservoir, Wapama Falls, and Kolana Rock laid out below. Two miles round trip. Go in spring when the falls are loud and the wildflowers are in.

properties.name: Taft Point
properties.blurb: A 2.2-mile round trip from the Sentinel Dome lot to a ledge 3,000 feet above the Valley floor, with The Fissures cracking the rim beside it. One railing, otherwise just you and the exposure. El Capitan straight across. Sunset here beats Glacier Point's crowd ten to one.

properties.name: Sentinel Dome
properties.blurb: The easiest 360-degree summit in the park. A 2.2-mile round trip from the same lot as Taft Point, ending on a bare granite dome with Half Dome, Yosemite Falls, and El Capitan in one slow turn. Full moon and star nights up here are the real show.

properties.name: Washburn Point
properties.blurb: The pullout just before Glacier Point, and the better angle on Vernal and Nevada Falls stacked in the Merced canyon. Most cars drive past it for the famous name a half mile on. Stop here first.

properties.name: Pothole Dome
properties.blurb: The low dome at the west end of Tuolumne Meadows. Twenty minutes of easy granite walking puts you above the whole meadow with the Cathedral Range behind it. The classic sunset spot in the high country, and the parking is a roadside shoulder, so arrive before the light does.

properties.name: Lembert Dome
properties.blurb: The big dome at the east end of the meadows. The trail wraps around the back for a walkable line to the top at 9,450 feet; the glacier-polished granite up there explains the whole landscape in one look. About 2.8 miles round trip from the lot off Tioga Road.

properties.name: Tuolumne Grove Trailhead
properties.blurb: Two dozen giant sequoias without the Mariposa Grove logistics. A mile down an old road from the Crane Flat lot, including a dead tree you can walk through, then the climb back out. No reservations, no shuttle, just go early for parking.

properties.name: Lukens Lake Trailhead
properties.blurb: The gentlest lake walk on Tioga Road. A mile and a half through fir forest to a quiet lake ringed by a wildflower meadow that peaks in July. Good first altitude leg stretch on the drive up, and a reliable corn lily show.
